coredata.dc:title: Efficient and visual monitoring of cerium (III) ions by green-fluorescent carbon dots and paper-based sensing
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Xiangqian
Zheng, Yuhui
Tang, Yiping
Chen, Qi
Gao, Jinwei
Luo, Qi
Wang, Qianming
coredata.dc:description: An efficient strategy for the synthesis of excitation-independent carbon nanodots has been provided. This functional nano-scale particle has been assembled by using citric acid and urea as raw materials through a microwave irradiation process in the absence of further surface modifications. The achieved nanomaterial demonstrates intensive green emissions and can be dispersible in aqueous environment. In particular, cerium (III) ion is able to quench its fluorescence emission intensity selectively and an “on-off” change has been observed. A good linearity between the concentration range of 10−6–10−4 M has been realized and the detection limit is determined to be 0.7 μM. More importantly, we report a color-evolution based paper sensor for effective monitoring of Ce3+ by incorporating fluorescent nanoprobe onto the cellulose paper substrate.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fluorescence
Sensor
Cerium

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of the carbon nanodots and the recognition process.
TEM (A) and DLS histogram of CQDs (B).
XPS spectrum (A) and high resolution C 1s, O 1s, and N 1s spectra of CQDs (B–D).

FT-IR spectra of citric acid (red line), urea (blue line) and prepared CQDs (green line). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
UV–Vis absorption spectra of Ce3+, CQDs and CQDs + Ce3+.

Emission spectra of CQDs upon addition of 10−6–10−4 M of Ce3+ in aqueous solution under excitation at 386 nm. Inset photo: CQDs solution excited by UV light at 365 nm without (left) and with (right) 100 μM of Ce3+.
Correlation between fluorescence intensity ratios (F0/F) and the concentration of Ce3+.

Emission spectra of CQDs in the presence of Ce3+ (0.1 mM) and different interfering analytes (1 mM), including(A) common cations (Al3+, Fe3+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Hg2+, Cu2+, Ni2+, Fe2+) and (B) analogous lanthanide ions (Pr3+, Nd3+, Pm3+, Sm3+, Eu3+, Gd3+, Tb3+, Dy3+, Ho3+, Er3+, Tm3+, Yb3+, Lu3+).

Fluorescence lifetimes of pure CQDs (red line) and in the presence of 100 μM Ce3+ (blue line). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

head.ce:title: Efficient and visual monitoring of cerium (III) ions by green-fluorescent carbon dots and paper-based sensing

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangzhou Hongqi Optical Instrument Technology Co. Ltd.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An efficient strategy for the synthesis of excitation-independent carbon nanodots has been provided. This functional nano-scale particle has been assembled by using citric acid and urea as raw materials through a microwave irradiation process in the absence of further surface modifications. The achieved nanomaterial demonstrates intensive green emissions and can be dispersible in aqueous environment. In particular, cerium (III) ion is able to quench its fluorescence emission intensity selectively and an “on-off” change has been observed. A good linearity between the concentration range of 10−6–10−4 M has been realized and the detection limit is determined to be 0.7 μM. More importantly, we report a color-evolution based paper sensor for effective monitoring of Ce3+ by incorporating fluorescent nanoprobe onto the cellulose paper substrate.

Green-emissive nano-scale particle has been assembled by using citric acid and urea as raw materials through a microwave irradiation process. Cerium (III) ion is able to quench its fluorescence emission intensity selectively and an “on-off” change has been observed.
Unlabelled Image

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are generally defined as nanoparticles with sizes less than 10 nm [1]. This nanoscale material is regarded to possess an amorphous/crystalline core with sp2 carbon as majority and an outside shell with a variety of functional groups. Under visible or ultraviolet irradiation, these rich oxygen-containing groups on the surface will endow the carbon dots to improve its photoluminescence properties [2]. They have attracted significant attention due to their outstanding properties, including high photostability, low toxicity, eco-friendly preparation and water solubility [3–6]. In this way, CQDs show a wide range of application such as bioimaging [7,8], chemical sensing [9–11], photocatalysis [12–14] and electrocatalysis [15,16].

Cerium(III) is normally referred to as one of the rare earth elements. In reality, it is more plentiful in the earth's crust than many other elements. It is also the most widely distributed among the “rare earths”, averaging 22 mg/kg in the earth crust [17,18]. Cerium is frequently used in luminescence, agriculture, catalysis, nuclear energy, metallurgy, microelectronics, therapeutic application, magnetism, glass and ceramics. Hence, it will be compulsory for the researchers to explore its environmental, medical and biological effects [19,20]. The current approaches such as inductively coupled plasma, ion-selective electrodes and potentiometric titration have a few weak points including high energy consumption, high cost and low repeatability [21–23]. Therefore, the availability of rapid, sensitive and selective methods for cerium determination is a real challenge.

Here, we have synthesized a fluorescent carbon dot material with green emissions. More strikingly, this nano-chemosensor can selectively detect Ce3+ ion in the aqueous solution. The luminescence changes can be realized with bare eye observation under the irradiation of a portable UV lamp. The proposed assay exhibits good sensitivity and a linear correlation. The detection limit has been shown to be 0.7 μM for Ce3+ ion. In principle, the nanosized fluorescent probe can be interacted with an analyte (here is Ce3+) to generate a “turned off” response. However, it still depends on specific instruments. To carry out the analysis with more convenience, fluorescent test paper has been designed since it possesses the simplicity and visualization capability for the detection with the assistance of a portable ultra-violet lamp. This contribution may offer a new channel for recognizing cerium ions by using the label-free carbon nanomaterials.

Citric acid (CA, AR) and Urea (99%) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Company. Deionized water and all the used salts were provided by Guangzhou Chemical Reagent Factory and used without any further purification.

TEM images were collected by a JEOL JEM-2100HR transmission electron microscope. FT-IR spectra were measured within the 3500–500 cm−1 wavenumber range by using a Prestige-21 spectrometer (Shimadzu) with the KBr pellet technique. UV–Vis spectra were recorded on an Agilent8453 spectrophotometer in the range of 200–500 nm with a slit of 2 nm. Fluorescence and excitation spectra were measured by using a Hitachi F-4600 fluorescence spectrophotometer with a 450 W xenon lamp as a light source. The fluorescence lifetime (τ) of CQDs was assessed by fluorescent decay curves. The fluorescent decay curve of CQDs was recorded at 524 nm under excited at 386 nm based on an Edinburgh FLS920 spectrometer. All the experiments were performed at room temperature. All error bars represent standard deviations from three repeated experiments. The quantum yield of CQDs was calculated by using Rodamine 6G as the standard. It was employed to obtain the quantum yield by using the following equation:
Ф =
Ф s
IA s
n 2
/
I s
An s
2

In equation, the subscript “s” refers to the standard. “Φ” is the fluorescence quantum yield, “A” is the absorbance, “η” is the refractive index of the solvent. “I” is integrated intensity. The absorbance should be below 0.05 in order to avoid inner filter effect.

CQDs were prepared according to literature procedure with slight modifications [24]. At first, citric acid (0.5 g) and urea (5 g) were added to deionized water (10 mL) in a 25 mL breaker to form a transparent solution under vigorous stirring for 30 min. The solution was heated under microwave irradiation for 3 min at 800 W. The solution was changed from colorless to brown at the first stage, then the black color appeared and the crude product of carbon dots was formed. After cooling down to room temperature, 10 mL deionized water was added to disperse the formed CQDs. the resultant solution was centrifuged at 12000 rpm for 10 min to remove large precipitates. For further purifying the CQDs, the resultant solution was dialyzed in a dialysis bag (molecular weight cutoff = 1000) for 3 days to remove the unreacted citric acid or urea. The collected solution in the dialysis bag (slightly viscous) was added dropwise to 50 mL ethanol to precipitate the carbon dots. The sample was washed twice with anhydrous ethanol and the remaining solvent was removed by freeze-drying treatment to achieve the powder material. The obtained CQDs were redispersed in 10 mL of deionized water and the sample was stored at 4 °C for future uses.

100 μL of the as synthesized CQDs solution was injected into pure water solution (900 μL) in a spectrophotometer quartz cuvette (0.05 mg/mL). Then a series of different concentrations of Ce3+ was added. The fluorescence spectra were collected after the addition of Ce3+ ion into the solution for 30 s. The spectra were measured by a fluorescence spectrophotometer excited at 386 nm. Except the concentration of 0.05 mg/mL (CQDs concentration), 0.01 mg/mL and 0.1 mg/mL were also chosen to study the detection performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of Paper-based Sensor for Visual Detection of Ce3+ Ions
ce:section.ce:para: To prepare paper-based fluorescence probe, the cellulose acetate paper with side length of 1.0 cm was immersed in the CQDs solution (0.1 mg/mL) and dried naturally. The achieved CQDs paper displayed green emissions under the excitation by a 365 nm UV lamp. To visualize Ce3+ ions in aqueous solution, the paper-based sensor was put into the Ce3+ ions containing solution (5 mL) with different concentrations (0, 25, 50 and 100 μM) under ambient condition. The emission response of the test paper could be clearly observed with the naked eye under the illumination by a UV lamp at 365 nm.

In Fig. 1 , CQDs were obtained from a single step of carbonization method based on citric acid as the molecular precursor. This pyrolytic reaction derived from carbohydrate source is readily available and efficient to obtain CQDs. Citric acid, an important organic acid, has been frequently used as a raw material for synthesizing CQDs. Moreover, the fluorescence quantum yield can be improved through surface passivation. Urea bears multiple amino groups and is considered as the effective passivation ligand to optimize the surface properties. This microwave assisted pyrolysis would stimulate the polymerization reaction between carboxyl groups of citric acid and amine moieties. The achieved polymer materials were further carbonized into carbon nanoparticles during the thermolysis process. The resultant deep brown powders indicated the successful carbonization of the raw materials. The obtained CQDs displayed excellent water-solubility and showed the potential for industrial applications. Normally, the functionalized amine groups play as the nitrogen doping precursor and passivating agents, which will be closely related to the photoluminescence performance. In the fluorescence measurements, the excitation peak wavelength of CQDs was located at 281 nm and 386 nm. The emission peak wavelength of CQDs was determined to be 524 nm and the green luminescence has been obtained. The quantum yield of the carbon dots has been calculated to be 18.7%. In previous literatures, the excitation dependence of emission peaks in carbon quantum dot has been reported [25–27]. However, here the emission curves under the excitations at two wavelengths (281 and 386 nm) were explored and the lines showed that the emission band has negligible difference (Fig. S1). This λex independent emission results might be ascribed to the uniform particle size and homogenous surface functionalization. As for the stability, we have explored emission signals at different periods of time. The reduction in emission intensities at 524 nm was less than 5% within seven days (Fig. S2). In addition, the photostability of the carbon dots upon the irradiation by ultra-violet light was also investigated and the results supported the nanomaterial maintained its stability under the continuous irradiation for 120 min (Fig. S3).

The microstructures were analyzed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Based on the collected images (Fig. 2A), CQDs obtained in this study are homogeneously dispersed and have spherical shape with a diameter of 2–4 nm which were consistent with the result of dynamic light scattering (DLS) histogram in Fig. 2B.

The full-scan X-ray photo electron spectroscopy (XPS) of CQDs was determined to explore their composition and chemical bonds (Fig. 3A). Three peaks, at 284.6, 395.3, and 527.1 eV, are attributed to C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s, respectively. The high resolution XPS spectra of C 1s, N 1s and O 1s (Fig. 3B, C and D) clearly supported the presence of CN, CO/CO, and NH bonds in CQDs. The nanomaterial was further clarified by Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy (Fig. 4 ). The strong vibrational stretching peak at 3179 cm−1 was attributed to the NH2 group and the results revealed that there are multiple NH2 on the surface of the CQDs. Different from pure citric acid and urea molecules, a group of peaks at 1694, 1618, 1448 and 1165 cm−1 were corresponding to the stretching vibrations of CO, CN, CN and CO bonds, respectively. These multiple hydrophilic groups (NH2 and COOH) demonstrated the potentials of excellent water dispersibility.

The presence of main functional groups in the nanostructure has also been supported by ultra-violet absorption studies (Fig. 5 ). In the absorption curve of CQDs, the weak band around 252 nm was ascribed to π-π* transitions of CC groups and the observed peak at around 270 nm was attributed to π-π* transitions of CC bond. The broad band at 333 nm would be assigned to the electron transitions of carbonyl moieties. Furthermore, an obvious absorption band at 410 nm has been detected and this intensive signal could be assigned to the n-π* transitions derived from lone pairs of nitrogen atoms in the nanodomains [28].

Interference study is also critical to evaluate the performance of a sensor. The influence of other cations has been investigated by the similar method. As given in Figs. 8 and S4, no obvious changes could be observed after the addition of analogous lanthanide ions (Pr3+, Nd3+, Pm3+, Sm3+, Eu3+, Gd3+, Tb3+, Dy3+, Ho3+, Er3+, Tm3+, Yb3+, Lu3+) and common cations (Al3+, Fe3+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Hg2+, Cu2+, Ni2+, Fe2+). These results firmly supported that the CQDs fluorescence sensor was selective for efficient recognition of Ce3+ in aqueous medium. A remarkably simple and efficient detection method can be established on the nanomaterial.

To further clarify the quenching mechanism, the absorption spectra and emission lifetimes of CQDs in the absence or presence of Ce3+ were measured and recorded. As shown in Fig. 9 , all the collected results were consistent with the single exponential decay curves. When 50 μM Ce3+ was added, no obvious changes can be observed. These results supported that the lifetimes of the CQDs remained to be relatively stable during the titration period. From the UV curves of the CQDs (Figs. S5 and S6), the enhanced absorption peak could be identified clearly in the presence of Ce3+. It may rule out the influence of molecular collisions and rely on the ground state changes. Accordingly, the “on-off” fluorescent changes would be referred to the static quenching process.

With the aim of preparing the test paper, the cellulose acetate paper has been immersed into the CQDs solution (0.1 mg/mL) for 30 min prior to assay. The incorporation of carbon dots has induced intensive green emissions. After the test paper was immersed into cerium solution, the striking luminescence color change with the dosage of Ce3+ could be seen under the UV lamp irradiations. As provided in Fig. S7, the addition of 100 μM of Ce3+ triggered the complete quench of emissions and the result was in a good agreement with the above photoluminescence studies. Additionally, the effects of the interference ions on the luminescence of the paper-based sensor were also recorded and the results also supported this optical sensor was only responsive to the presence of cerium ions (Fig. S8).

To verify the performance of carbon dots in real application, the cerium concentration in river water was explored. The cerium contents in the samples were evaluated by the emission changes and the working curve. As given in Table S2, the recovery percentage was determined to be in the range of 96.9–106.2% and the values were satisfactory. The collected results demonstrated that the paper-based carbon dots sensor could be promising for quantitative detection of cerium in real environment.

In summary, we have successfully fabricated stable and fluorescent CQDs in aqueous solution. The diameters of the synthesized CQDs were mainly distributed in the range of 2–4 nm. The as-derived CQDs can be used as excellent fluorescent probe for Ce3+ ions assay. The interactions of CQDs with interference cations and related lanthanide ions were studied by fluorescence spectroscopy. The proposed strategy displays the following advantages: I. it is very simple and straightforward technique to prepare fluorescent CQDs with efficient treatments. II. The prepared CQDs show great stability. III. The construction of the Ce3+ ions sensor is very facile and low-cost without any requirement for chemical labeling and modification of probes. IV. A paper-made sensor containing the green-emissive species has been assembled for practical detection of Ce3+. In this case, the study will stimulate the development of future work concerning simple synthesis of fluorescent nanomaterials and extend their application in biomedical, ecological and photochemical fields.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Q.M. appreciates National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21371063), Natural Science Foundation of Guangdong (2015A030313378) and Guangdong Science and Technology plan (2016A050502053). J. W. thanks supports from National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51571094) and Innovation team project by Department of Education of Guangdong Province (2016KCXTD009). Y.H. is grateful to the Research Fund of Higher Education Committee of Guangdong Province (GDJ2016003). Q. Luo thanks for the grant of Scientific Research Fund from Guangzhou City (No. 201802010032). X.Q. appreciates for the financial support from Graduate School of South China Normal University (2017LKXM044).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2018.08.021.
